text: Artificial intelligence researchers at DeepMind have developed a new algorithm called AlphaFold 3 that can predict the structure of virtually all molecules in the human body with unprecedented accuracy. The system, described in a paper published in Nature, extends the capabilities of its predecessor AlphaFold 2 by modeling not just proteins but also DNA, RNA, and small molecules like drugs. The breakthrough could dramatically accelerate drug discovery by allowing scientists to computationally test how potential medicines interact with biological targets before conducting expensive laboratory experiments. In initial tests, AlphaFold 3 outperformed existing specialist tools on five out of eight molecular interaction benchmarks. Pharmaceutical companies including AstraZeneca and Eli Lilly have already begun integrating the technology into their research pipelines. DeepMind is making the system available to academics through a free online server, though commercial users will need to negotiate a license. Critics note that experimental validation of predicted structures remains essential, and that the tool works less reliably on novel molecule types not well represented in its training data.
reference_summary: DeepMind's AlphaFold 3 can predict the structure of proteins, DNA, RNA, and drug molecules with high accuracy, potentially speeding up drug discovery. It outperformed existing tools on most benchmarks and is available free to academics, though commercial use requires licensing.

text: Global average temperatures in 2023 reached 1.45 degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels, the highest ever recorded and approaching the critical 1.5-degree threshold set by the Paris Agreement, according to the European Union's Copernicus Climate Change Service. Every month of 2023 set a new monthly temperature record, a streak that continued into early 2024. Scientists attribute the record heat to the combination of long-term warming driven by greenhouse gas emissions and a strong El Niño weather pattern in the Pacific Ocean. The consequences were felt worldwide: Canada experienced its worst wildfire season on record, destroying more than 18 million hectares; Mediterranean Europe and North Africa endured extended heat waves with temperatures exceeding 45 degrees Celsius in some locations; and catastrophic flooding struck Libya, killing more than 10,000 people. Sea surface temperatures also hit unprecedented highs, threatening coral reef systems globally and disrupting fisheries that billions of people depend on for food. Climate scientists warn that without dramatic reductions in carbon emissions within this decade, temporary breaches of the 1.5-degree threshold will become permanent.
reference_summary: 2023 was the hottest year on record, reaching 1.45°C above pre-industrial levels, driven by greenhouse gas emissions and El Niño. Consequences included record Canadian wildfires, European heat waves, Libyan floods, and unprecedented ocean temperatures, with scientists warning that sustained 1.5°C warming requires urgent emissions reductions.

text: The United States Federal Reserve held its benchmark interest rate steady at a 23-year high of 5.25 to 5.5 percent for the sixth consecutive meeting, signaling that policymakers need more evidence that inflation is durably returning to their 2 percent target before beginning to cut rates. Fed Chair Jerome Powell acknowledged that inflation has eased considerably from its peak of 9.1 percent in June 2022 but noted that it remained above target at 3.2 percent as of the most recent reading. Financial markets had anticipated up to six rate cuts in 2024 at the start of the year, but those expectations have been scaled back to two or three following stronger-than-expected inflation data in the first quarter. Higher rates have cooled the housing market significantly, with 30-year mortgage rates hovering near 7 percent, the highest level since 2001. However, the labor market has remained resilient, with unemployment at 3.9 percent and employers continuing to add jobs at a solid pace. Economists are divided on whether the Fed can achieve a so-called soft landing, slowing inflation without triggering a recession.
reference_summary: The Federal Reserve kept interest rates at a 23-year high of 5.25-5.5%, awaiting further proof inflation is returning to the 2% target. Despite cooling from a 9.1% peak to 3.2%, strong early-2024 inflation data has reduced expected 2024 rate cuts from six to two or three, while housing has slowed but the labor market remains resilient.

text: A large clinical trial involving more than 45,000 participants across 18 countries has found that a combination therapy of two existing drugs reduces the risk of death from heart disease by 23 percent compared to standard treatment. The trial, sponsored by the World Health Organization and published in The Lancet, tested a fixed-dose pill combining a blood pressure medication and a cholesterol-lowering statin. Participants who received the combination pill were significantly more likely to adhere to their medication regimen — 87 percent compliance compared to 64 percent for those taking the drugs separately — which researchers believe accounts for much of the benefit. The treatment costs less than one dollar per day in low-income countries where generic versions are available, making it potentially accessible to hundreds of millions of people at high cardiovascular risk who currently receive no treatment. Researchers cautioned that not all patients are suitable candidates and that medical supervision is required to monitor for side effects. The WHO plans to update its treatment guidelines to recommend the combination therapy within the next six months.
reference_summary: A 45,000-person WHO-sponsored trial found combining a blood pressure drug and statin in a single pill reduces heart disease death risk by 23%, largely through improved adherence (87% vs 64%). Costing under $1/day in generic form, it could benefit millions in low-income countries, with WHO guideline updates expected within six months.

text: Negotiators from the United States and China concluded three days of high-level economic talks in Geneva with a joint statement agreeing to temporarily reduce tariffs imposed during the trade war that began under the Trump administration. The United States agreed to lower tariffs on Chinese goods from 145 percent to 30 percent for an initial 90-day period, while China agreed to reduce its retaliatory tariffs on American products from 125 percent to 10 percent and to lift non-tariff barriers that had restricted American agricultural and pharmaceutical exports. Markets reacted positively, with the S&P 500 rising 3.2 percent on the day of the announcement. Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen called the agreement a significant step toward stabilizing the economic relationship between the two countries, while cautioning that fundamental disagreements over trade practices, technology transfer, and national security remained unresolved. Chinese officials described the talks as constructive but emphasized that China would not compromise on issues it considers core national interests. Analysts noted that the 90-day window was intended to create space for more comprehensive negotiations but warned that structural tensions between the world's two largest economies are unlikely to be resolved quickly.
reference_summary: The US and China agreed to temporarily slash tariffs — US cuts from 145% to 30%, China from 125% to 10% — for 90 days following Geneva talks. Markets rallied 3.2%, but officials on both sides acknowledged fundamental disputes over trade practices and technology remain unresolved.
